title: Eclampsia at 35 Weeks with Magnesium Sulfate Toxicity After Loading Dose
subtitle: Magnesium Is the Seizure Prophylaxis, Calcium Gluconate Is the Antidote — Reflexes, Respirations, and Urine Output Are the Three Things You Check at Every Pass

patient.name: T. Nguyen-Hartwell (G2P1 at 35+2 weeks)
patient.admitDate: Today, 0915 (L&D triage arrival via husband for severe headache + vision changes; witnessed eclamptic seizure at 0942)
patient.room: L&D Bed 3 → OR pending emergent cesarean for non-reassuring fetal heart tracing + maternal stabilization

vitals.time: 1030 (CURRENT — 30 min into magnesium maintenance after 4 g loading dose; new hyporeflexia, drowsy, RR 10)

labs.n: Serum magnesium level (stat — drawn 1025 after 30 min into maintenance infusion)
labs.r: THERAPEUTIC range for seizure prophylaxis in eclampsia/preeclampsia: 4–7 mEq/L (5–8 mg/dL); toxicity 7–10 mEq/L (loss of DTRs at ~10); 10–13 (respiratory depression); >13 cardiac arrest; nonpregnant normal 1.7–2.4

labs.n: Reflex assessment (the most important clinical assay)
labs.v: Patellar (knee jerk) reflex ABSENT bilaterally; biceps 1+; pre-magnesium reflexes were 3+ (mildly hyperreflexic from preeclampsia)
labs.r: DTR monitoring during magnesium infusion is the CLINICAL antidote-decision tool — loss of patellar reflex precedes respiratory depression by minutes-to-hours, signaling stop-the-mag time. Document q1h during infusion; reassess after any rate change

labs.v: Pre-mag RR 22; 15 min into loading dose RR 18; 30 min into maintenance RR 10 (current); clinically appears mildly drowsy + sleepy
labs.r: Respiratory rate is a key magnesium toxicity vital sign — target ≥12/min; <12 = toxicity warning; <8 = severe toxicity requiring respiratory support; mag depresses respiratory drive AND smooth muscle of respiratory accessory muscles

labs.v: Foley catheter placed at 0950; UOP 25 mL in 40 min (= ~38 mL/hr) — borderline; pre-mag UOP not measured (no catheter yet)
labs.r: MAGNESIUM is RENALLY EXCRETED; UOP <30 mL/hr = relative oliguria → mag accumulates → toxicity at the same infusion rate. UOP <100 mL in 4 hr = the typical cutoff for reducing or holding maintenance

labs.v: Tonic-clonic generalized seizure x 90 seconds at 0942 in L&D triage room; preceded by complaint of severe headache and seeing 'flashing spots'; no incontinence, brief tongue laceration, postictal x 8 min with confusion; one episode, no recurrence; on continuous fetal monitor throughout
labs.r: ECLAMPSIA = SEIZURE in pregnancy/postpartum without other identified cause, in setting of preeclampsia (HTN + proteinuria/end-organ); can occur antepartum (50%), intrapartum (20%), or postpartum (30%, with peak in first 48 hr but up to 6 weeks); seizure prophylaxis post-event is mandatory

labs.v: BP 178/118 (severe ≥160/110); proteinuria 4+ on dipstick (severe ≥5 g/24 hr quantitative); platelets 88 (low); AST 142 (elevated); LDH 380; creatinine 1.1; severe headache; visual changes (flashing spots = scotomata); brisk reflexes pre-mag (3+); no pulmonary edema currently; mild RUQ tenderness; estimated dry weight 3+ kg gain over baseline
labs.r: Severe preeclampsia features (ANY ONE of): BP ≥160/110, plt <100, AST/ALT >2x upper limit, Cr >1.1 or doubling, persistent severe headache or visual changes, pulmonary edema, severe RUQ pain. Eclampsia (seizure) overrides feature counting — proceed with full treatment regardless of count

labs.v: Loading dose 4 g IV over 20 min started 0950, completed 1010; maintenance 2 g/hr IV infusion started 1010 (currently running); total Mg given so far ~4.7 g (4 g load + 0.7 g of maintenance at 30 min mark)
labs.r: STANDARD eclampsia/severe preeclampsia regimen: LOAD 4–6 g IV over 15–20 min, then MAINTENANCE 1–2 g/hr IV continuous; alternative IM regimen (5 g each buttock then 5 g IM q4h) used in low-resource settings; continue for 24 hr after delivery or 24 hr after last seizure (whichever later)

labs.v: Pre-seizure: Category I (reassuring) — baseline 145, moderate variability, accelerations present; during seizure: prolonged deceleration to 80 lasting 6 min (expected during maternal seizure from uteroplacental compromise); since seizure: tachycardia 175, MINIMAL variability, recurrent LATE DECELERATIONS — Category III (non-reassuring → emergent delivery indication)
labs.r: NICHD 3-tier FHR system: Cat I normal, Cat II indeterminate, Cat III abnormal (recurrent late decels with absent variability or sinusoidal — emergent delivery). The seizure caused acute uteroplacental compromise; recovery is expected but late decels post-event signal need for emergent delivery

labs.v: 35+2 weeks by certain LMP and confirmed by 1st trimester ultrasound; estimated fetal weight 2.4 kg by Leopold and recent growth ultrasound — appropriate for gestational age; head down; NICU notified for late preterm delivery (expect transient respiratory distress, possible RDS, thermoregulation, glucose monitoring)
labs.r: 35–36 weeks = LATE PRETERM (34–36+6); generally good outcomes but with elevated risk of respiratory issues, hypoglycemia, hypothermia, feeding difficulties, jaundice. Antenatal steroids (betamethasone 12 mg IM q24h x 2 doses) ideally completed >24 hr before delivery — NOT possible here given the emergent indication; neonatology aware

labs.v: G2P1: 1st pregnancy 3 yr ago — normal SVD at 39 weeks with mild gestational HTN (not preeclampsia); no chronic HTN; no prior preeclampsia; type 2 DM screened negative; BMI 32 (Class I obesity — risk factor); no prior surgeries; no family history of preeclampsia (per intake); irregular prenatal care this pregnancy — missed last 2 visits ('busy with work, didn't think it mattered')
labs.r: Risk factors for preeclampsia: nulliparity (vs multipara — she's multip but with prior gestational HTN), prior preeclampsia, chronic HTN, DM, obesity, advanced maternal age >35, family history, autoimmune (lupus, APLS), kidney disease, IVF/donor egg, multiple gestation. Inadequate prenatal care = missed BP trends

labs.v: CBC: WBC 14.2, Hgb 10.8, Plt 88 (low — preeclampsia/HELLP feature); BMP: Na 132, K 4.0, Cl 102, HCO3 22, BUN 14, Cr 1.1 (mildly elevated from pregnancy baseline ~0.7), glucose 92; LFTs: AST 142, ALT 96, total bili 0.8, LDH 380 (elevated; HELLP if ≥600); haptoglobin pending; coags: INR 1.0, fibrinogen 380 (normal); UA: 4+ protein, no nitrites/LE
labs.r: HELLP syndrome features partially present: hemolysis pending, ELEVATED LIVER ENZYMES present (mild), LOW PLATELETS present — partial HELLP variant. Could progress; monitor q4–6h; concerning for DIC if fibrinogen drops or INR rises

labs.v: Severe-range BP 178/118 → IV LABETALOL 20 mg IV push given at 0945; if BP not <160/110 in 10 min, repeat 40 mg; if not, repeat 80 mg; alternatives: hydralazine 5–10 mg IV slow push, nifedipine 10 mg PO; AVOID ACE inhibitors (teratogenic) and nitroprusside (cyanide toxicity in prolonged use)
labs.r: Target BP 140–155 / 90–105 (NOT 'normal' — over-correction reduces uteroplacental perfusion); labetalol contraindicated in asthma and bradycardia (this patient OK); hydralazine alternative for asthma; nifedipine alternative oral

labs.v: No pulmonary edema on lung auscultation (clear bilaterally); no abruptio placentae signs (no vaginal bleeding, no uterine tetany); no DIC clinically (no oozing from IV sites, fibrinogen 380); no focal neuro deficit post-seizure (rules out stroke); no history of seizure disorder
labs.r: Helps narrow differential of the seizure to eclampsia (most likely) rather than stroke, intracranial hemorrhage, epilepsy, hypoglycemia (glucose 92 — ruled out), illicit drug-induced

nursesNote: 1030 — 28 y/o G2P1 at 35+2 weeks presented to L&D triage at 0915 via husband for severe headache and 'flashing spots' in vision; BP on arrival 178/118 (severe range), 4+ proteinuria, platelets 88, AST 142, mild RUQ tenderness — SEVERE PREECLAMPSIA. At 0942 patient had a witnessed generalized TONIC-CLONIC SEIZURE lasting ~90 seconds in the triage room — ECLAMPSIA. Maintained airway during seizure with left-side positioning, suction at bedside, supplemental O2 by NRB; postictal 8 min with confusion then alert. During the seizure the continuous fetal monitor showed prolonged deceleration to 80 lasting 6 min (expected acute uteroplacental compromise during maternal seizure). Since seizure, FHR pattern has been NON-REASSURING (Category III): baseline tachycardia 175, MINIMAL variability, recurrent LATE decelerations — emergent cesarean section being prepared, anesthesia at bedside, OR being readied. MAGNESIUM SULFATE seizure prophylaxis initiated immediately after seizure stabilization: 4 g IV loading dose over 20 min (0950–1010), then 2 g/hr IV maintenance infusion started 1010 (currently running); also given LABETALOL 20 mg IV push at 0945 for the severe-range BP (BP improved to 162/104). At 1030 (30 min into maintenance infusion), bedside reassessment per protocol revealed concerning changes: PATELLAR REFLEX ABSENT bilaterally (was 3+ pre-magnesium); biceps 1+; RESPIRATORY RATE 10 (down from 18); patient drowsy; complaining of warmth and flushing (common at therapeutic levels but excessive here). Stat mag level drawn at 1025 returned 9.4 mEq/L — TOXIC RANGE. UOP 25 mL in 40 min via foley = ~38 mL/hr (borderline; magnesium is renally excreted, so reduced UOP → accumulation). IMMEDIATELY STOPPED THE MAGNESIUM INFUSION; CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1 g (10 mL of 10% solution) IV slow push over 3 min started — the antidote for magnesium toxicity; reassessing reflexes, RR, mentation every 5 min initially. Notified attending OB physician (already at bedside for cesarean prep), anesthesia, NICU. The competing tensions: she needs seizure prophylaxis (mag) to prevent recurrent eclamptic seizure, AND she has mag toxicity (need to hold or reduce). Plan after calcium gluconate: monitor; once reflexes and RR recover, plan to RESTART maintenance magnesium at REDUCED RATE (1 g/hr) given her oliguria; continue close monitoring of reflexes, RR, UOP, level; remember magnesium continues postpartum (24 hr after delivery OR 24 hr after last seizure — whichever later). Continuous fetal monitoring; OR ready for emergent cesarean; foley to gravity (mag-mandated for accurate UOP monitoring); IV access x 2 (18g R AC, 20g L forearm); type & crossmatch 2 units PRBC and 2 FFP. Husband at bedside, very anxious but supportive; speaking with the chaplain. NICU on standby for late preterm delivery — antenatal betamethasone not feasible given emergent indication; neonate may need brief respiratory support, glucose monitoring, thermoregulation. Postpartum eclampsia risk remains for up to 6 weeks (peaks first 48 hr) — continue mag prophylaxis post-cesarean for 24 hr, then close BP monitoring, education on warning signs (severe headache, visual changes, RUQ pain, decreased urine output, swelling).
providerOrders: EMERGENT CESAREAN SECTION for Category III FHR (recurrent late decels with minimal variability post-eclamptic seizure) and severe preeclampsia/eclampsia in 35+2 wk gestation; anesthesia at bedside; OR ready; NICU notified

MAGNESIUM SULFATE seizure prophylaxis: LOAD 4–6 g IV over 15–20 min (DONE), MAINTENANCE 1–2 g/hr IV continuous; continue for 24 HR AFTER DELIVERY OR 24 HR AFTER LAST SEIZURE, whichever later; restart at REDUCED rate (1 g/hr) given current oliguria after mag toxicity resolves

MAGNESIUM TOXICITY (CURRENT EVENT — level 9.4, absent patellar reflex, RR 10): (1) STOP infusion immediately, (2) CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1 g (10 mL of 10%) IV slow push over 3 min — the ANTIDOTE; antagonizes magnesium at the neuromuscular junction; effect within minutes

Magnesium MONITORING — q1h during infusion: (1) DEEP TENDON REFLEXES (patellar primary; biceps if epidural in place); (2) RESPIRATORY RATE (target ≥12/min; <12 = toxicity warning; <8 = stop and antidote); (3) URINE OUTPUT (target ≥30 mL/hr; <100 mL in 4 hr = reduce or hold maintenance); (4) Serum level q4–6h or with any clinical change
Magnesium therapeutic range 4–7 mEq/L; toxicity progression: 7–10 mEq/L loss of DTRs → 10–13 respiratory depression → >13 cardiac arrest; clinical findings track ahead of the lab value

ANTIHYPERTENSIVE for severe-range BP (≥160/110): LABETALOL 20 mg IV push (DONE); if BP not <160/110 in 10 min: repeat 40 mg, then 80 mg (max 220–300 mg total); ALTERNATIVES: HYDRALAZINE 5–10 mg IV slow push q15–20 min (preferred in asthma); NIFEDIPINE 10 mg PO short-acting (NOT sublingual which causes precipitous drops); target BP 140–155 / 90–105 (NOT 'normal' — over-correction reduces uteroplacental perfusion)
AVOID ACE inhibitors, ARBs (teratogenic — fetal renal toxicity, oligohydramnios, skull defects); AVOID nitroprusside (cyanide toxicity in prolonged use, fetal cyanide)
Continuous fetal heart rate monitoring; reassess FHR pattern q15 min during stabilization and during transport to OR
Continuous maternal cardiac monitoring; pulse oximetry; q15-min vitals during active treatment then q1h
Two large-bore IV accesses (≥18 g); type & crossmatch 2 units PRBC + 2 FFP; have on standby
Foley catheter to gravity (mag-mandated for accurate UOP monitoring during infusion); STRICT I&O hourly; UOP target ≥30 mL/hr
NPO for cesarean delivery; H2 blocker (famotidine 20 mg IV) for aspiration prophylaxis; sodium citrate 30 mL PO non-particulate antacid immediately pre-op
Position LEFT LATERAL TILT (10–15° wedge under right hip) to displace gravid uterus off IVC and aorta — improves cardiac output and uteroplacental perfusion

AIRWAY READY for seizure recurrence or worsening respiratory depression from mag — bag-mask, suction, oral airway, ET tube; anesthesia at bedside; consider IV access for emergent intubation drugs (succinylcholine for RSI — NOTE: succinylcholine MAY HAVE PROLONGED EFFECT IN MAGNESIUM TOXICITY due to NMJ interaction; cisatracurium or rocuronium may also be prolonged)

Seizure recurrence management: if seizure during mag infusion (breakthrough), give additional MAG SULFATE 2–4 g IV over 5–10 min; if persistent, escalate to LORAZEPAM 2–4 mg IV or LEVETIRACETAM 60 mg/kg IV; NEVER give phenytoin first-line in eclampsia (less effective than mag); manage airway, position left lateral, oxygen, document

Labs: STAT magnesium q4–6h or with clinical change; CBC, CMP, LFTs, coags, fibrinogen, LDH, haptoglobin, type & crossmatch q6h until stable (watch for HELLP/DIC progression); urine protein/creatinine ratio

NICU at delivery — late preterm 35+2 wk with non-reassuring FHR; expect transient respiratory issues; antenatal betamethasone NOT FEASIBLE given emergent indication; neonatal resuscitation team at delivery

Postpartum: CONTINUE magnesium for 24 hr after delivery (or 24 hr after last seizure, whichever later); BP monitoring q1h x 4 hr, q2h x 24 hr, q4h until discharge; warning signs education before discharge (severe headache, visual changes, RUQ pain, decreased UOP, severe swelling, chest pain, dyspnea); follow-up BP check at 3–7 days postpartum; 6-week postpartum visit
POSTPARTUM ECLAMPSIA RISK: up to 6 WEEKS postpartum, with PEAK in first 48 hr; education and warning-sign awareness; outpatient BP self-monitoring; PCP and OB f/u
Anti-D (RhoGAM) postpartum if mother Rh-negative (verify type)
VTE prophylaxis postpartum: pneumatic compression devices intraop and continued postop; LMWH (enoxaparin) once not actively bleeding (preeclampsia + cesarean + obesity = elevated VTE risk)
Family support: husband present; chaplain available; social work; lactation consult for postpartum (mother may face delayed milk-coming-in due to preterm + cesarean — encouragement and pump support)

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: MAGNESIUM TOXICITY: serum level 9.4 mEq/L (therapeutic 4–7), ABSENT patellar reflex bilaterally, RR 10, drowsy, flushed — clinical + lab confirmation
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — drug toxicity in progress. STOP the infusion IMMEDIATELY; give CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1 g IV slow push (antidote). Reflex loss precedes respiratory depression — recognize early. Mag toxicity progression: loss of DTRs → respiratory depression → cardiac arrest at very high levels.

opts.text: Eclamptic seizure (witnessed tonic-clonic x 90 seconds) in a patient with severe preeclampsia (BP 178/118, 4+ proteinuria, platelets 88, AST 142, mild HELLP features)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — eclampsia defines need for magnesium seizure prophylaxis + delivery planning. Severe preeclampsia criteria already met pre-seizure; eclampsia (seizure) confirms the most severe form. Risk of recurrence without prophylaxis is high; mag must continue (at reduced rate after toxicity resolves).

opts.text: Non-reassuring FHR pattern (Category III): baseline tachycardia 175, minimal variability, recurrent late decelerations — post-seizure uteroplacental compromise
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — EMERGENT CESAREAN INDICATION. Category III FHR = abnormal = expedited/emergent delivery. Position mother left lateral tilt, O2 by NRB, IV fluid bolus, prepare OR, NICU notified.

opts.text: Severe-range BP 162/104 (down from 178/118 after labetalol) — still above the severe threshold of 160/110
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — additional antihypertensive needed. Target BP 140–155 / 90–105 (NOT 'normal'); over-correction reduces uteroplacental perfusion. Repeat labetalol 40 mg if BP remains ≥160/110 in 10 min; can escalate to 80 mg; or alternative hydralazine 5–10 mg IV slow.

opts.text: Oliguria UOP ~38 mL/hr — magnesium is renally excreted; reduced clearance = accumulation at the same infusion rate
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — explains the toxicity development. Magnesium clearance is renal; UOP <30 mL/hr or <100 mL in 4 hr should prompt reducing or holding maintenance. Patient was at borderline UOP, and her renal function compromised from preeclampsia (Cr 1.1) — predictable accumulation.

opts.text: Preeclampsia with HELLP features: platelets 88, AST 142 — partial HELLP
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — may progress; monitor q4–6h for full HELLP, DIC, hepatic dysfunction. Delivery is treatment for HELLP/preeclampsia; postpartum monitoring continues for at least 48 hr (HELLP can worsen postpartum).

opts.text: Late preterm 35+2 wk delivery imminent — NICU notified; antenatal betamethasone not feasible given emergent indication
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — neonatal considerations. Late preterm at risk of respiratory distress, hypoglycemia, hypothermia, feeding difficulty, jaundice. Neonatal resuscitation team at delivery. Mother–baby separation likely for NICU admission.

opts.text: Risk of postpartum eclampsia: up to 6 weeks postpartum, peaks first 48 hr — continued magnesium prophylaxis for 24 hr post-delivery
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — sustained care planning. Many providers and patients think 'baby out = problem solved' — wrong. Postpartum eclampsia is real and avoidable with continued mag and BP monitoring. Discharge education on warning signs is critical.

opts.text: Irregular prenatal care — missed last 2 visits; BP trend was rising (146/96 at last visit 4 days ago)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — modifiable risk factor for future pregnancy. Preeclampsia surveillance requires regular BP and urine monitoring; missed visits lose the trend. Patient education on importance of prenatal care; address barriers (work, transportation, childcare).

opts.text: Husband at bedside, supportive, very anxious
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — family support and communication. Anxious partner during obstetric emergency benefits from clear updates, chaplain support, and inclusion in decision-making when possible. Chaplain available; social work as needed.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA
opts.rat: Reassuring — no drug allergies. Document and proceed.

opts.text: Temperature 98.2°F
opts.rat: Reassuring — rules out infection / fever as confounding factor. Not the primary issue.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What is happening pharmacologically and pathophysiologically?
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pharmacology / pathophysiology.

opts.text: ECLAMPSIA = seizure in a pregnant or postpartum patient (within 6 weeks) without other identified cause, in the setting of preeclampsia (HTN + proteinuria/end-organ); pathophysiology = endothelial dysfunction, cerebral vasospasm, hypertensive encephalopathy, possibly PRES (posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome)
opts.rat: CORRECT — definition and mechanism. Eclampsia can occur antepartum (50%), intrapartum (20%), postpartum (30%); postpartum eclampsia peaks first 48 hr but can occur up to 6 weeks. Delivery is the definitive treatment but does not eliminate immediate risk.

opts.text: MAGNESIUM SULFATE is the FIRST-LINE seizure prophylaxis AND treatment in eclampsia; mechanism likely via NMDA receptor antagonism, cerebral vasodilation, calcium channel modulation; superior to phenytoin and diazepam in obstetric trials (Magpie, Eclampsia Trial Collaborative); maternal AND fetal safety profile well-established
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield OB pharm. Standard dosing: LOAD 4–6 g IV over 15–20 min, MAINTENANCE 1–2 g/hr IV continuous; continue 24 hr post-delivery or 24 hr after last seizure (whichever later). NEVER give phenytoin or benzo as first-line in eclampsia — less effective.

opts.text: MAGNESIUM TOXICITY progression: loss of deep tendon reflexes at ~7–10 mEq/L → respiratory depression at 10–13 → cardiac arrest at >13; CLINICAL findings track AHEAD of the lab value; monitor REFLEXES (patellar), RESPIRATIONS (rate), URINE OUTPUT (renal excretion) at every assessment pass
opts.rat: CORRECT — the three-check rule. Bedside clinical assessment is the most reliable real-time toxicity detection. Reflex loss precedes respiratory depression by minutes-to-hours. Document the trifecta q1h during infusion; reassess after any rate change or change in clinical status.

opts.text: CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1 g IV slow push over 3 min is the ANTIDOTE for magnesium toxicity — directly antagonizes magnesium at the neuromuscular junction and cardiac membrane; effect within minutes; can be repeated if needed; have at bedside during any mag infusion
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield. Calcium gluconate (peripheral OK) over 3 min, NOT push too fast (cardiac risk). 1 g (10 mL of 10%) is standard initial; can repeat. Calcium chloride (3x more elemental Ca) only via central line (vesicant). The unique exception to 'don't give calcium for hyperK in dig toxicity' — here, calcium IS the answer.

opts.text: Magnesium is RENALLY EXCRETED; reduced UOP (<30 mL/hr or <100 mL in 4 hr) = accumulation at the same infusion rate; preeclampsia patients often have reduced renal function — monitor UOP closely and reduce mag rate if oliguric
opts.rat: CORRECT — explains the predictable toxicity pattern. Patient had borderline UOP 38 mL/hr with mildly elevated Cr 1.1 — accumulation was inevitable. After toxicity resolves, restart mag at reduced rate (1 g/hr instead of 2) given renal compromise.

opts.text: Eclampsia/severe preeclampsia BP target: 140–155 / 90–105 — NOT 'normal'; over-correction reduces uteroplacental perfusion. First-line IV: LABETALOL 20 mg → 40 → 80 (max 220–300); HYDRALAZINE 5–10 mg slow push (preferred in asthma); NIFEDIPINE 10 mg PO short-acting (NEVER sublingual)
opts.rat: CORRECT — BP target rationale. Severe HTN >160/110 = stroke risk in mother → treat; moderate HTN <160/110 acceptable. Labetalol contraindicated in asthma/bradycardia; hydralazine for asthma. NEVER short-acting nifedipine sublingually (precipitous drop, hypotension, fetal distress).

opts.text: ANTI-HYPERTENSIVES TO AVOID IN PREGNANCY: ACE inhibitors and ARBs (teratogenic — fetal renal toxicity, oligohydramnios, skull defects, IUGR); nitroprusside (cyanide toxicity in prolonged use, fetal cyanide); atenolol associated with growth restriction
opts.rat: CORRECT — drug safety in pregnancy. Methyldopa, labetalol, hydralazine, nifedipine are mainstays. ACE inhibitors are absolutely contraindicated 2nd-3rd trimester; some teratogenic effects in 1st trimester too. Document and educate.

opts.text: Definitive treatment for severe preeclampsia/eclampsia is DELIVERY; magnesium provides seizure prophylaxis bridging to delivery; antihypertensives manage BP; once delivered, the disease resolves over weeks (BP often elevated for 6 weeks postpartum; education on outpatient management)
opts.rat: CORRECT — only the placenta is the cure. Delivery timing balances maternal severity vs fetal maturity. Severe preeclampsia or eclampsia at ≥34 weeks → deliver. <34 weeks → individualize based on stability + betamethasone. At 35+2 weeks here, delivery is clearly indicated.

opts.text: Phenytoin (Dilantin) 20 mg/kg IV is the FIRST-LINE drug for eclampsia seizures
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Phenytoin is less effective than magnesium in eclampsia (multiple trials showed mag superior at reducing recurrent seizures). MAGNESIUM SULFATE is first-line. Phenytoin reserved for refractory cases or contraindications to mag. NCLEX trap if you generalize 'phenytoin for seizures.'

opts.text: Continue magnesium at the same rate (2 g/hr) despite the toxicity — the seizure prophylaxis benefit outweighs the risks
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUSLY WRONG. Toxicity at the current rate will progress to respiratory arrest. STOP, give calcium gluconate, then RESTART at REDUCED RATE (1 g/hr) once recovered. The competing tensions (seizure prevention vs toxicity) are managed by reducing rate, not continuing toxic levels.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Risk for Respiratory Arrest r/t magnesium toxicity (level 9.4, RR 10, absent reflexes) — imminent if not antidoted
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Mag toxicity progression to respiratory arrest is the imminent killer. STOP infusion + calcium gluconate IV is the immediate response. Airway equipment ready; anesthesia at bedside; reassess RR, reflexes, mentation in minutes.

opts.text: Risk for Fetal Compromise / Demise r/t Category III FHR pattern with recurrent late decels, post-seizure uteroplacental compromise
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. EMERGENT cesarean indicated. Position left lateral tilt, O2 by NRB, IV fluid bolus, OR ready, NICU notified. Fetal status dictates delivery urgency; maternal stabilization in parallel.

opts.text: Risk for Recurrent Eclamptic Seizure r/t severe preeclampsia, peripartum window; need for continued magnesium prophylaxis once toxicity resolves
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Mag remains the seizure prophylaxis; restart at reduced rate after toxicity recovers. Continue 24 hr post-delivery or 24 hr after last seizure. Maintain seizure precautions; airway readily accessible; benzodiazepines available for breakthrough.

opts.text: Severe Hypertension r/t severe preeclampsia BP 162/104 still above severe threshold — stroke risk
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Treat to 140–155 / 90–105 (NOT 'normal'). Repeat labetalol 40 mg if BP ≥160/110 in 10 min; can escalate to 80 mg; or hydralazine alternative. Avoid over-correction (uteroplacental perfusion).

opts.text: Knowledge Deficit / Family Distress r/t complex emergent diagnosis, fear for mother and baby, planned cesarean and NICU admission
opts.rat: #5 — Important but lowest in immediate physiologic stack. Clear communication with husband; chaplain; social work; later education on postpartum eclampsia risk + warning signs + outpatient monitoring + future pregnancy considerations.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: STOP magnesium sulfate infusion immediately; CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1 g (10 mL of 10%) IV slow push over 3 min — the antidote
opts.rat: Immediate reversal of magnesium toxicity. Antagonizes Mg at the neuromuscular junction and cardiac membrane. Effect within minutes. Can be repeated if needed. Have at bedside during any mag infusion.

opts.text: Continue magnesium at the same rate (2 g/hr) and add lorazepam 4 mg IV to prevent recurrent seizure
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUSLY WRONG. Continuing toxic levels leads to respiratory arrest. Stop, antidote, restart at REDUCED rate. Benzodiazepines additionally suppress respiratory drive in an already-mag-depressed patient — combined depression is dangerous.

opts.text: Once magnesium toxicity resolves (DTRs return, RR ≥12), RESTART maintenance at REDUCED rate 1 g/hr (not 2 g/hr) given oliguria
opts.rat: Resumption of seizure prophylaxis at adjusted rate. Renal function dictates rate. Continue 24 hr post-delivery or 24 hr after last seizure (whichever later). Reassess UOP and reflexes q1h.

opts.text: EMERGENT CESAREAN section for non-reassuring FHR + severe preeclampsia/eclampsia at 35+2 wk; anesthesia, OR, NICU coordinated
opts.rat: Definitive treatment for the eclampsia and the fetal compromise. Cesarean (rather than vaginal) given the Category III FHR and the maternal severity. Anesthesia choice typically regional if feasible; general if rapid airway needed or contraindication to neuraxial.

opts.text: LABETALOL 40 mg IV push if BP remains ≥160/110 after the 20 mg dose; alternative HYDRALAZINE 5–10 mg slow push if labetalol contraindicated; target 140–155 / 90–105
opts.rat: Sequential dose escalation for severe-range BP. Target maintains uteroplacental perfusion while preventing maternal stroke. Avoid over-correction. AVOID ACE inhibitors and nitroprusside in pregnancy.

opts.text: Sublingual nifedipine 10 mg for rapid BP lowering
opts.rat: ⚠️ Sublingual nifedipine causes precipitous BP drops with severe maternal hypotension and fetal distress; NEVER use sublingually. Oral short-acting nifedipine 10 mg PO is an acceptable alternative when needed; IV labetalol or hydralazine preferred.

opts.text: Phenytoin 20 mg/kg IV loading dose as first-line for seizure prophylaxis
opts.rat: ⚠️ Less effective than magnesium for eclampsia seizure prevention (Magpie and Eclampsia Trial). Mag is first-line. Phenytoin reserved for refractory cases or mag contraindications.

opts.text: Left lateral tilt positioning (10–15° wedge under right hip) to displace gravid uterus off IVC and aorta; supplemental O2 by NRB
opts.rat: Improves cardiac output and uteroplacental perfusion. Acute intervention for non-reassuring FHR. Continue during transport to OR. Supplemental O2 supports both maternal and fetal oxygenation.

opts.text: Foley catheter to gravity for accurate UOP monitoring during magnesium infusion (target ≥30 mL/hr)
opts.rat: Mag-mandated monitoring — UOP is one of the three checks (reflexes, RR, UOP). Allows real-time detection of oliguria → mag accumulation. Continues postpartum during the 24-hr mag continuation.

opts.text: Two large-bore IVs; type & crossmatch 2 units PRBC + 2 FFP; have on standby; airway equipment + anesthesia at bedside
opts.rat: Pre-operative readiness for emergent cesarean. PRBC for surgical losses; FFP if HELLP/DIC progresses. Airway equipment for potential respiratory failure from mag toxicity OR for emergent intubation for cesarean.

opts.text: NICU notification for late preterm 35+2 wk delivery with non-reassuring FHR; antenatal betamethasone (cannot be administered given the emergent indication)
opts.rat: Neonatal team at delivery. Late preterm risks: respiratory distress, hypoglycemia, hypothermia, feeding difficulty, jaundice. Betamethasone would benefit if there were 24+ hr; emergent delivery precludes.

opts.text: Postpartum continuation of magnesium for 24 hr after delivery (or 24 hr after last seizure, whichever later); BP monitoring q1h x 4 hr then q2h x 24 hr; education on postpartum eclampsia warning signs
opts.rat: Postpartum eclampsia risk extends to 6 weeks with peak 48 hr. Continued mag and BP monitoring. Discharge education: severe headache, visual changes, RUQ pain, decreased UOP, severe swelling, chest pain, dyspnea — return for ANY.

opts.text: Anti-D (RhoGAM) postpartum if mother is Rh-negative; VTE prophylaxis with pneumatic compression and LMWH once not actively bleeding
opts.rat: Standard postpartum cesarean care. Verify Rh status; RhoGAM within 72 hr if Rh- mother with potentially Rh+ baby. VTE prophylaxis essential — preeclampsia + cesarean + obesity = elevated VTE risk.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: STOP magnesium infusion immediately; CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1 g IV slow push over 3 min; airway equipment + anesthesia at bedside; reassess RR, reflexes, mentation q5 min
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. The mag toxicity is the imminent killer. Stop the cause; antidote the effect; have airway support ready. Calcium gluconate effect within minutes. Document time, dose, response.

opts.text: Left lateral tilt position; supplemental O2 by NRB; IV fluid bolus 500 mL NS; reassess FHR pattern; expedite OR transport for emergent cesarean
opts.rat: #2 — Fetal status interventions in parallel. Left lateral tilt + O2 + fluid bolus are the bedside interventions to improve uteroplacental perfusion while OR is being readied. FHR may improve; if not, expedite cesarean.

opts.text: Repeat LABETALOL 40 mg IV push for the BP 162/104 (still ≥160/110); reassess BP in 10 min; alternative HYDRALAZINE 5–10 mg if needed; target 140–155 / 90–105
opts.rat: #3 — BP control while readying for delivery. Target not 'normal' but below severe threshold. Document; reassess; can escalate to 80 mg labetalol if needed.

opts.text: Once magnesium toxicity resolves (DTRs return, RR ≥12, more alert): RESTART maintenance magnesium at REDUCED rate 1 g/hr given oliguria; continue close mag monitoring (reflexes, RR, UOP, level q4–6h)
opts.rat: #4 — Resumption of seizure prophylaxis at adjusted rate. Continue 24 hr post-delivery or 24 hr after last seizure (whichever later). Reduced rate accounts for the oliguria contributing to the toxicity event.

opts.text: Coordinate OR + anesthesia + NICU + neonatology for cesarean delivery; communicate with husband; chaplain; postpartum plan with continued mag, BP monitoring, education on postpartum eclampsia warning signs
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained care + family + delivery coordination. Postpartum: continue mag, BP monitoring q1h x 4 hr then q2h x 24 hr, q4h until discharge. Discharge education on warning signs and outpatient follow-up at 3–7 days postpartum.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: 10 min after calcium gluconate + mag infusion stopped: patellar reflex 2+ bilaterally; biceps 2+; RR 16; alert and oriented x3; flushing resolving; reassess level pending
opts.rat: Antidote effective. Magnesium toxicity reversed at the receptor level. Mag level will trend down over hours. Plan to restart mag at REDUCED rate 1 g/hr once stable; continue cesarean preparation; reassess UOP and renal function.

opts.text: Post-cesarean (90 min after toxicity event): live infant female 2.3 kg, Apgar 7/8 (mild respiratory distress, transferred to NICU on HFNC), mother stable with BP 142/88 on labetalol IV, mag at 1 g/hr running with reflexes 2+ RR 18 UOP 50 mL/hr
opts.rat: Successful delivery and stabilization. Mother on the postpartum eclampsia/preeclampsia path. Baby in NICU for late preterm care. Continue mag x 24 hr post-delivery; BP monitoring; communicate with NICU; lactation support for pumping if not directly nursing.

opts.text: Discharge day 4: BP 134/82 on PO labetalol (transitioned from IV); off magnesium; CBC, LFTs, Cr trending toward normal; baby still in NICU on minimal support; postpartum eclampsia education completed with husband; outpatient BP follow-up at 5 days; 2-week and 6-week postpartum visits scheduled
opts.rat: Comprehensive recovery. Continued outpatient management. Education on warning signs: severe headache, visual changes, RUQ pain, decreased UOP, severe swelling, chest pain, dyspnea — return for ANY. Lactation support continued; mental health screening for postpartum mood; follow-up echo if cardiac concerns.

opts.text: 5 min after calcium gluconate: reflexes still absent, RR 8, decreasing mentation, SpO2 89% — calcium NOT yet effective or insufficient response
opts.rat: Inadequate response. Repeat calcium gluconate 1 g IV slow push; prepare for intubation; bag-mask ventilation if needed; anesthesia at bedside. Cardiac monitoring throughout. Continue mag held; cesarean expedited.

opts.text: Hour 6 postpartum: BP 188/120 (severe-range again); severe headache returning; visual changes; reflexes 4+ — postpartum eclampsia warning
opts.rat: Postpartum preeclampsia/eclampsia recurrence. Continue mag (now restart with another loading dose if needed); IV labetalol or hydralazine for BP; preparedness for postpartum seizure (60% of postpartum eclampsia occurs within first 24 hr). Continue mag x 24 hr from this point.

opts.text: Day 2 postpartum: HELLP labs worsen with platelets 42, AST 280, LDH 880, hemolysis on smear; coagulation derangement INR 1.6, fibrinogen 180 — full HELLP with possible DIC
opts.rat: Progression to full HELLP +/- DIC. Postpartum HELLP can worsen for 48–72 hr before improving. Supportive care: platelet transfusion if active bleeding or for procedure, FFP/cryo if fibrinogen <100, monitor for end-organ dysfunction (hepatic rupture rare but catastrophic). Hematology consult if needed.

opts.text: Postpartum eclampsia teaching: husband verbalizes warning signs to watch for at home (severe headache, visual changes, RUQ pain, decreased UOP, severe swelling); writes down outpatient follow-up appointments; comfortable using home BP cuff
opts.rat: Successful education outcome. Postpartum eclampsia risk up to 6 weeks — preparedness matters. Husband as informed family member improves home monitoring; home BP cuff for daily checks; clear written instructions; follow-up appointments scheduled (5 days, 2 weeks, 6 weeks).

opts.text: 6-week postpartum visit: BP 122/76 off all antihypertensives; baby thriving at home (discharged from NICU at 2 weeks); patient asks about future pregnancy risk
opts.rat: Full recovery. Counseling on future pregnancy: ~20% recurrence risk for preeclampsia (higher if severe/early-onset); recommended LOW-DOSE ASPIRIN 81 mg PO daily starting in 1st trimester for prophylaxis; calcium supplementation if intake low; preconception counseling; close prenatal care; refer for maternal-fetal medicine if planning another pregnancy.

opts.text: BP 142/86 on PO labetalol at 6 weeks postpartum; continuing antihypertensive; PCP follow-up established for ongoing HTN management
opts.rat: Persistent postpartum hypertension — common. Some women have residual HTN for weeks-to-months postpartum or develop chronic HTN unmasked by pregnancy. Continue antihypertensive; PCP for ongoing management; reassess at 12 weeks postpartum; cardiovascular risk factor reduction long-term.

opts.text: Baby remains in NICU at week 2 for late preterm feeding and thermoregulation; mother pumping with success; bonding strong; discharge planning underway
opts.rat: Expected NICU course for late preterm with non-reassuring FHR delivery. Feeding and thermoregulation are common late-preterm issues. Mother pumping maintains supply; skin-to-skin and feeding involvement encouraged; lactation consult; NICU social work for family support and discharge planning.
